title: POS vs Payment Service: What Does a Store Actually Need?
metaTitle: POS vs Payment Service: What Does a Store Actually Need?
description: Understand the different roles, costs, integration points, duplicate-entry risks, and implementation order for POS and payment services.
lead: A POS system and a cashless payment service are not the same product. POS manages products, tax, inventory, customers, and sales records. A payment service authorizes cards, e-money, and QR transactions and settles funds. Many stores need both systems connected.
author: Luqevora Editorial Team

sources.label: Airレジ — 機能・できること
Airペイ — 決済手数料・振込手数料
Airペイ — 決済手数料ディスカウントプログラム

sections.heading: Verdict: most modern stores need both roles
body: Unless a store is cash-only and needs only very simple records, POS and payment functions remain distinct. A store managing product sales or inventory while accepting cashless methods usually combines both.
Even when one provider offers both roles, as Square does, understand the POS software and payment contract as separate cost and operational components.

sections.heading: What the POS manages
body: A POS manages catalog, prices, tax, discounts, returns, staff, inventory, customers, sales reporting, and register closing.
Restaurant systems can add tables, ordering, kitchen flow, and mobile ordering around the POS record.

rows: Products, tax, checkout, and sales data
Software, devices, printers, cash hardware, modules
AirREGI, Square POS, funfo, Kantan Chumon

sections.heading: What the payment service manages
body: A payment service handles reading, customer verification, authorization, cancellation, processing fees, merchant underwriting, and settlement.
Compare brand-level rates, deposit cadence, bank transfer fees, terminals, connectivity, and chargeback operations.

sections.heading: Problems without integration
body: Manually entering the POS amount into a payment terminal increases input errors, missed cancellations, and differences between POS and payment totals.
Amount transfer and payment-result integration can reduce duplicate entry and reconciliation, but the exact supported model and software version must be confirmed.

sections.heading: Implementation order
body: Define the operating requirements, shortlist POS systems, and confirm formally supported payment services before contracting. This sequence reduces integration surprises.
When the payment terminal is selected first, verify future POS compatibility, cancellation charges, and minimum terminal term.

sections.heading: Build a complete cost sheet
body: Keep POS and payment costs in separate columns and then consolidate them into first-year and three-year totals.

Treat the POS and payment service as two connected controls. Define which system is the sales record, how amounts are transferred, how partial refunds and cancellations are reconciled, and who resolves mismatches. During a pilot, test duplicate entry, offline operation, receipt reprints, tax corrections, and end-of-day totals rather than evaluating only a successful standard payment.
bullets: Tablets, dedicated terminals, printers, and cash hardware
Processing, monthly, connectivity, and transaction fees

faqs.question: Should I simply choose the lowest processing rate?
faqs.answer: No. Compare the effective cost including eligibility, payment mix, hardware, subscriptions, settlement, and integration.

faqs.question: Should POS and payments come from one provider?
faqs.answer: One provider can simplify amount transfer and support, but also compare functionality, rates, and portability.

faqs.question: Which sales data is needed for a comparison?
faqs.answer: Prepare at least three months of value and transaction count by card brand, e-money, QR, cash, and in-person or online channel.
